concepts.title: Binomial Theorem for fractional indexes
concepts.conceptBody:
Binomial theorem for fractional index 

For any rational number $\text{n}$,

${{(1+\text{x})}^{\text{n}}}=1+\text{nx}+\frac{\text{n}\left( \text{n}-1 \right)}{1.2}{{\text{x}}^{2}}+\frac{\text{n}\left( \text{n}-1 \right)\left( \text{n}-2 \right)}{1.2.3}{{\text{x}}^{3}}+\ldots \infty \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ provided }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left| \text{x} \right|<1.$

The restriction on $\text{x}$ is not required when $\text{n}$ is a natural number.

Now, we shall see that when $\text{n}$ is a natural number, then the above expansion coincides with that as given earlier.

Let $\text{n}\in \text{N}$ and $\left| \text{x} \right|<1$, then we have

$(1 + x^n) = 1 + nx + \frac{n(n-1)}{1\cdot 2}x^2 + \frac{n(n-1)(n-2)}{1\cdot 2\cdot 3}x^3 + \ldots$

$~+\frac{\text{n}\left( \text{n}-1 \right)\left( \text{n}-2 \right)\ldots \left( \text{n}-\left( \text{n}-1 \right) \right)\left( \text{n}-\text{n} \right)}{1.2.3\ldots .\left( \text{n}+1 \right)}{{\text{x}}^{\text{n}+1}}+\ldots \ldots $

$~={{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{0}}{{1}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{x}}^{0}}+{{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{1}}{{1}^{\text{n}-1}}{{\text{x}}^{1}}+{{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{2}}{{1}^{\text{n}-3}}{{\text{x}}^{3}}+\ldots \ldots +{{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{n}}}{{1}^{0}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{n}}}+0+0\ldots $

$~={{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{0}}{{1}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{x}}^{0}}+{{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{1}}{{1}^{\text{n}-1}}{{\text{x}}^{1}}+{{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{2}}{{1}^{\text{n}-2}}{{\text{x}}^{2}}+{{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{3}}{{1}^{\text{n}-3}}{{\text{x}}^{3}}+\ldots ..+{{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{n}}}{{1}^{0}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{n}}}$

This is the same expansion as would have given by the binomial theorem for positive integral index.

Some Observations 

  • If $\text{n}\in \text{N}$, then ${{(1+\text{x})}^{\text{n}}}$ is defined for all values of $\text{x}$ and if $\text{n}\in \text{NQ}-\text{N}$, then ${{(1+\text{x})}^{\text{n}}}$ is defined only when $\left| x \right|<1$.
  • If $\text{n}\in \text{N}$, then ${{(1+\text{x})}^{\text{n}}}$ contains only $\text{n}+1$ terms and if $\text{n}\in \text{Q}-\text{N}$, then ${{(1+\text{x})}^{\text{n}}}$ contains infinitely many terms.
  • In the expansion of ${{(1+\text{x})}^{\text{n}}}$, the exponent of $\text{x}$ goes on increasing through 0 .
  • If $\text{n}\in \text{N}$, then the coefficient of any term in ${{(1+\text{x})}^{\text{n}}}$ is ${{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{k}}}$ where $\text{k}$ is the exponent of $\text{x}$.
  • If $n\in N$, then the exact value of ${{(1+x)}^{n}}$ can be found by adding all terms (equal to $\left. n+1 \right)$ in the expansion of ${{(1+x)}^{n}}$ and if $n\in Q-N$, then only an approximate value of ${{(1+x)}^{n}}$ can be found by adding certain finite number of terms in the expansion of ${{(1+x)}^{n}}$.

Working rules for expanding \[{{(1+x)}^{n}},n\in Q\] 

Step 1: (a) If $n\in N$, then ${{(1+x)}^{n}}$ can be expanded for all values of x and has (n + 1) terms. 

            (b) If $\text{n}\in \text{Q}-\text{N}$, then ${{(1+\text{x})}^{\text{n}}}$ can be expanded only when $\left| \text{x} \right|<1$ and has infinitely many terms.

Step 2 : The first term in ${{(1+\text{x})}^{\text{n}}}$ is always 1.

Step 3 : The second terms is the product ' $\text{nx}$ ' of $\text{n}$ and $\text{x}$.

Step 4 : For the third term, take coefficient as $\frac{n\left( n-1 \right)}{1.2}$ increase the power of $x$ by 1 . Thus, the third term is $\frac{\text{n}\left( \text{n}-1 \right)}{1.2}{{\text{x}}^{2}}$. Repeat this process repeatedly.

Negative binomial series

The series which arises in the binomial theorem for negative integer $(-n$.)

${{(\text{x}+\text{a})}^{-\text{n}}}$ $~=\underset{\text{k}=0}{\overset{\infty }{\mathop \sum }}\,\left( \begin{matrix}
-\text{n} \\
\text{k} \\
\end{matrix} \right){{\text{x}}^{\text{k}}}{{\text{a}}^{-\text{n}-\text{k}}}$ (1)
$~=\sum ~{{(-1)}^{\text{k}}}\left( \begin{matrix}
\text{n}+\text{k}-1 \\
\text{k} \\
\end{matrix} \right){{\text{x}}^{\text{k}}}{{\text{a}}^{-\text{n}-\text{k}}}$ (2)

for $\left| \text{x} \right|<\text{a}$.

For $\text{a}=1$, the negative binomial series simplifies to

${{(\text{x}+1)}^{-\text{n}}}=1-\text{nx}+\frac{1}{2}\text{n}\left( \text{n}+1 \right){{\text{x}}^{2}}-\frac{1}{6}\text{n}\left( \text{n}+1 \right)\left( \text{n}+2 \right){{\text{x}}^{3}}+\ldots $

Expand $\frac{2-x}{\left( 1-x \right)\left( 3-x \right)}$ in ascending powers of x and find ${{x}^{r}}$. Also state the range of x for which this expression is valid. 

Solution: 

Given expression $=\frac{2-x}{\left( 1-x \right)\left( 3-x \right)}$

On expressing RHS in the form of partial fractions, we get

Given expression $=\frac{1}{2\left( 1-x \right)}+\frac{1}{2\left( 3-x \right)}$

$\Rightarrow $ Given expression $=\frac{1}{2}{{(1-\text{x})}^{-1}}+\frac{1}{6}{{\left( 1-\frac{\text{x}}{3} \right)}^{-1}}$

Using the expansions of ${{(1-x)}^{-1}}$, we get

Given expression $=\frac{1}{2}\left( 1+\text{x}+{{\text{x}}^{2}}+{{\text{x}}^{3}}+\ldots \ldots \ldots \right)$

$~+\frac{1}{6}\left( 1+\frac{\text{x}}{3}+\frac{{{\text{x}}^{2}}}{9}+\frac{{{\text{x}}^{3}}}{27}+\ldots \ldots \ldots \right)$

$~\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ Given }\!\!~\!\!\text{ expression }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }=\left( \frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{6} \right)+\left( \frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{18} \right)\text{x}$

$~+\left( \frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{54} \right){{\text{x}}^{2}}+\ldots \ldots +\left( \frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{{{6.3}^{\text{r}}}} \right){{\text{x}}^{\text{r}}}+\ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots $

$\Rightarrow $ Given expression $=\frac{2}{3}+\frac{5}{9}\text{x}+\frac{14}{27}{{\text{x}}^{2}}+\ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots +\frac{1}{2}\left( 1+\frac{1}{{{3}^{\text{r}+1}}} \right){{\text{x}}^{\text{r}}}+\ldots \ldots \ldots $

Coefficient of ${{\text{x}}^{\text{r}}}=\frac{1}{2}\left( 1+\frac{1}{{{3}^{\text{r}+1}}} \right){{\text{x}}^{\text{r}}}$

Since ${{(1-\text{x})}^{-1}}$ is valid for $\text{x}\in \left( -1,1 \right)$ and ${{(1-\text{x}/3)}^{-1}}$ is valid for $\text{x}\in \left( -3,3 \right)$, the given expression is valid for $\text{x}\in \left( -1,1 \right)$ (i.e. take intersection of the two sets)

Hence $\frac{2-x}{\left( 1-x \right)\left( 3-x \right)}$ is valid for $-1<x<1$.

examples.title: The first four terms in the expansion of $(1-x)^{3 / 2}$ are
The fourth term in the expansion of $(1-2 x)^{3 / 2}$ will be
$\frac{1}{\left(x^2+\frac{1}{x}\right)^{\frac{4}{3}}}$ can be expanded by binomial theorem, if
